front: Keyboard Collection: A collection of 42 keyboard works dedicated to 'My Ladye Nevell,' containing ten paired pavanes and galliards
Keyboard Collection: Contains a nine-movement battaglia called 'The Battle,' whose fourth section includes 'the marche of horsemen' and 'the flute & the drome'
Keyboard Collection: Contains variations on 'Sellinger's Round' and 'Carman's Whistle'; alongside the Fitzwilliam Virginal Book, the most important English Renaissance keyboard collection
Motet Collection: Co-published with Thomas Tallis and dedicated to Queen Elizabeth I, after the two composers were granted a 21-year music printing monopoly
Motet Collection: 109-piece collection providing complete Mass Propers for the major feasts of the Roman liturgy; published by a Catholic composer under a Protestant regime
Motet: Written in G Aeolian mode; unusually stresses 'verum' rather than 'corpus'; uses a false relation between F-sharp in the Superius and F in the Bassus
Mass: This composer wrote three complete Mass settings, for three, four, and five voices respectively
Composer: Wrote the elegy 'Ye Sacred Muses' on this composer's death, ending 'Tallis is dead, and Music dies'
Composer: Student of Thomas Tallis; together they held a 21-year royal monopoly on printing polyphonic music granted by Queen Elizabeth I
Composer: Catholic composer who prospered under Protestant Queen Elizabeth I; organist at the Chapel Royal in London
Composer: Wrote the earliest known hexachord fantasia, preceding John Bull and Jan Sweelinck
work: Earl of Salisbury Pavans / Keyboard Works

front: Composer: Composed a pair of dances inspired by the death of Robert Cecil, titled for the 'Earl of Salisbury'
work: Earl of Salisbury Pavans / Keyboard Works

front: Composer: Taught Thomas Morley, author of Plain and Easy Introduction to Practical Music; known as 'Britanniae Musicae Parens'
Keyboard Collection: Contains the song 'Will You Walk the Woods so Wild' and a hexachord fantasia for the virginal
Motet: Opening chords are G minor, D major, then unexpectedly F major; ends with a false relation on 'Miserere mei' between a tenor F-sharp and bass F-natural
Motet: Setting of a 14th-century Latin Eucharistic hymn whose opening words translate as 'Hail, true body'; composed in G minor and 4/2 time
Motet Collection: Resulted from the first-ever letters patent on music printing, granted by Elizabeth I
Composer: Contributed two pavanes and four galliards to Parthenia, the first printed collection of keyboard music in England
